little advice from an Italian : if you go for dinner at 6PM in Italy (what you define "normal time" but for me it would be like snack time!!) it's like having a sign on your face that says "I'm english or american, and I don't know anything about good italian food"

The only places to eat that are serving food at that time in Italy are 100% tourist traps, with reasonably awesome food, not always italian, mostly american-like, and high prices (for the most, naturally)

"When in Rome...."

Stress yourself a little bit, do what Italians do and have an aperitivo at 6pm, not a dinner, and wait until 8pm, better 8,30 or 9, to get the REAL deal

If a place gives you food at 6PM is a place where no italians will ever go, and guess what... italians do know one thing or two about godd old (and proper) italian food... ;-)

For instance we NEVER get a plate with some olive oil to be eaten with the bread, this is actually (I think) an american thing, but if you go there at 6pm, they know where you are from and they try to give you the best american or british experience to make you feel comfortable. If you are ok with that, that's fine, but if you are here for an italian experience, there's NOTHING italian in a plate with some olive oil, and this is just one little example
Tipping 5% or any percentage? Never seen in Italy... definitely an touristy thing. They know you are american or british and they charge 5%, and that seems a little for you. I will NEVER eat in a place where the service

I grew up in Rome .... Until my generation, people could quite easily  climb over the fences and entering the Colyseum at night. You could find many people inside every night .... People smoking on the upper stands, people playing guitar, people just hanging around, and people like my friends and I who played hide-and-seek in the underground tunnels, where gladiators used to wait before going to the stage. Where this researcher was at the beginning of the video.

Unforgettable experience,
even if, for us romans, it seemed almost a normal thing to do

As late as globalization took over, that means until 90s, Rome, despite being a 3-4 milion-person city, kept an atmosphere almost as a village.
You used to feel totally safe, people care about each other, and also police controls weren't strict.
A social agreement that went destroyed with the globalization.

About the Coliseum, don't dare anymore to enter it at night .... you would get arrested.

It's important to highlight that the gladiatorial fight depicted in this video appears hugely inaccurate in the historic terms. First of all, there are no historic evidences which prove that Verus fought in the style of MVRMILLO, nor that Priscus appeared as THRAEX. Second thing is equally important, as each gladiatorial fight has been supervised by the referee, known as SVMMA • RVDIS - he payed a special attention to the combat rules and punished every "illegal move". Several referees were being present in case of collective or simultaneous gladiatorial fights. MVRMILLONES and THRAECES needed to keep their helmets firmly at their heads all the time during the arena fights - that was the important rule of distinction. One of the reasons for such approach laid down in their representation of various mythological creatures, and these were considered as the important aspects of the "divine beyondness". Such hierarchical expression of the universal order needed to remain sheltered and honored even during the arena bloodbaths - after all, gladiatorial fights originally appeared as the inseparable segments of the funerary festivities and their divine rituals. Generally speaking, gladiators were always dressed in the traditional arms and armor of their classes. They fought by using the various weapon types (some of the arena fights were being carried out with the wooden armament), and were not allowed to perform fistfight or wrestling activities once being disarmed. Over the years, I've been to Rome a dozen times for work.  Ive been to many other European hot spot cities as well.  Rome is the only place I would spend my own money to go back to.  

The Metro system there is fantastic and super easy to figure out.   I always stayed within a few blocks of El Centro - where the main Metro Hub is.  From there, you can go anywhere easily and the hotels are fine and not as expensive as it is within walking distance of the historic district.


For beach lovers take the Metro to Osteo Lido, its the last Metro train stop going west.  Get off and walk half a mile to the black sand beach.  Mind your feet.  That black sand is super hot, even on just a mildly warm sunny day. (ask me how I know).

There are some great restaurants, but they can be pricey.  I had the best steak meal of my life there, (cant remember the name of the restaurant tho).  For the budget minded, or if you're pressed for time, the food at the Pizza Rustica places is great and a lot less expensive than the cafes and restaurants - a LOT less expensive! I love me some Pizza Rustica! 

For those craving to sit at a table on the sidewalk and have a refreshment while people watching -  The same coffee sitting at a sidewalk table will cost twice what it does while standing. IMO, its worth it though.  Dont expect the waiter to come check on you.  He won't.  If you need him, or her, just wave them over.

My personal favorite handout is Piazza Novona at dusk.  Not overly crowded like Trevi

@sagedakotalmft7763  I answer to you and to everybody that says "but if I eat so late..."

My comment was not meant to say to anyone what to do. everyone has his own reasons to do or not to do things, and I totally respect anyone.

If you want real italian food, however, it's really hard to find it at 6PM. That's a fact.
What can you do to get the real deal at 6PM? Nothing, I guess... But you can "sacrifice" a couple of hours of sightseeing the next day and go to bed a little later.
You can have sightseeing at 8AM or real food, but you can't have both.

The italian way?
We eat late, we sleep late, we wake up early and sometimes we take a nap after lunch... (btw that's why between 2pm and 4pm most italians are not around)

Not your way?
I totally understand, it's a little weird, but still the way we do things.

REAL italian food after 1PM for lunch and after 8 PM for dinner.
That's a fact, as I told you.

You can adjust your daily schedule, or get touristy food. It's up to you.
What you can't have is keep your daily schedule AND have proper italian food.

Whatever you choose, I'm fine.
I just wanted to inform you all about how things work down here ;-)

I own the original DoW version and although I enjoyed the overall game back then I am overall almost harsher on it than a 3.5 / 5 (it's a 6/10 for me on BGG). Don't know about the rule changes, but this is the issue that impacted the rating very negatively for me:
The starting tiles (event assets or performers as they are called in this version I guess?) and events all players receive can hugely impact the game especially if playing with less than 5 players. You see, if someone initially gets tokens that don't match their own event and are not even desirable for trades by other players (due to the whole set not being dealt out), it's very easy to get an early loser.
In a rare twist for Euro-Games there is (or was, if it hasn't been removed) also a partial run-away mechanic instead of a catch-up rubberbanding where the player with the highest score receives a podium at the end of a round that will benefit them in the next rounds. And again, it's much easier for lucky players to achieve this early lead than the ones who got screwed by the initial setup even without too much trading.

Two things:
(1) That Roman cement wasn't a grand secret, but more like (I think) a trade secret. Cement was everywhere, and it can't be everywhere if it is a grand secret, but there's this story that persists today that Roman cement was lost and no one knows how it was made. A tour guide in Roman told our group that after Rome fell (and therefore so did the trade networks bringing in raw materials, and perhaps guild records too), cement would be made by burning Roman marble to make the quicklime. The knowledge wasn't completely lost. 
(2) How the Roman republic encountered the same difficulty that we do today, that representation of the people is considered a threat by...the people. They want "the best" (that is, "aristocracy", however that may be described, but often equates to valiant warrior), and they end up voting for people who don't represent them, and then resent them for not representing them, and omigoshhowdidwegetanemperor?!

Just a small note: The Tutankhamun coffinette you showed was the one that contained his lungs, not his liver. The only real way to tell them apart is by the text running down the front, and that mentions Hapy, 𓐑𓊪𓇋𓇋 not Imsety 𓐝𓊃𓍘𓇋.

Also, while the Tutankhamun coffinette you showed for reference wears a nemes headdress (with the pointy bits on the side, just like he wears in his famous mask), the Duamutef coffinette doesn’t. He wears a tripartite wig. If you see a god or a goddess with a headdress that falls down over their shoulders, usually it’s a tripartite wig, especially if they’re animal headed. Nemes headdresses are usually worn by kings, although you will see (usually human headed) gods sometimes wearing them, like Osiris in the tomb of Pashedu, and in some bronze figures of Harpocrates.

The man who stole the Mona Lisa was an Italian called Vincenzo Peruggia (1881-1925), an amateur painter who worked at the Louvre. On the 21st of August 1911, he took the painting out of its frame and, putting it under his uniform, a large white blouse, walked out of the building. Two years later he tried to sell it to the director of the Uffizi Museum in Florence. The director went to see him with two policemen who arrested Peruggia. The thief did not resist and said he just wanted to return the painting to Italy where it belonged. He served 7 months in prison and died twelve years later of a heart attack.

When I stayed in Paris around 30 years ago I visited the Louvre three times, spending a day in each wing.

One of the  best decisions I made was getting the "Amis De Louvre" membership. You get to use the group entrance, so you are in the galleries at 9:02 and I was able to get 10-15 minutes alone looking at the Venus De Milo. It's 80 Euro. It also gets you around the timed ticket requirements. I went 8 times in 2 weeks, and did not see everything. If you are planning on going 2x the time saved in line plus the costs breaks even. The gallery closures that are scheduled throughout the week are important if there is a specific work, or subject area you want to spend time in. They were moving sculpture the second week I was there, and there is a spectacular piece I was too intimidated to try and sketch and then when I went back twice the gallery was closed.
